question_title: Will a 1kt meteor strike take place during March or April 2026?
summary:
The question asks whether a ≥1 kt impact energy meteor will appear in the NASA Fireball Database between March 10 and May 1, 2026. The March 17, 2026 Ohio meteor (widely covered in news) had only 0.37 kt impact energy per the NASA API — well below the threshold. GalaxOS erroneously displayed it as 12.6 kt by confusing the radiated energy field with impact energy.

Historical base rate from the NASA Fireball Database: ~3.1-3.5 events ≥1 kt per year (118 events since 1988, 22 since 2020). Using a Poisson model over the remaining ~43-day window (March 18 to April 30), the probability of at least one qualifying event is 30-34%. Minor downward adjustments for database reporting lag (late April events might not appear by May 1) bring the estimate to ~32%.

factors.description: Historical base rate: ~3.1-3.5 events ≥1kt per year in NASA Fireball Database (118 events in 38 years, 22 in recent 6.2 years). Poisson model gives ~30-34% for 43 remaining days in the resolution window.

No qualifying event yet in window: March 17 Ohio meteor had only 0.37 kt impact energy per NASA API (below 1 kt threshold). GalaxOS display of 12.6 kt was an error confusing radiated energy with impact energy.
Current 211-day gap since last ≥1 kt event is somewhat unusual (14.7% probability under Poisson). However, Poisson memoryless property means this doesn't affect forward probability.
Database reporting lag: question resolves May 1, so very late April events may not be recorded in time. Effective window may be ~40 days rather than 43.
No seasonality: March/April accounts for 16.1% of historical events vs 16.7% expected if uniform. No adjustment needed.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 32% probability (YES)**

Key findings:
- **Base rate**: The NASA Fireball Database records ~3.1-3.5 events ≥1 kt per year. Over the remaining ~43 days in the resolution window, the Poisson probability of at least one qualifying event is **30-34%**.
- **March 17 Ohio meteor does NOT qualify**: Despite widespread media coverage and GalaxOS displaying "12.60 kt," the NASA API shows the actual total impact energy was only **0.37 kt** — the "12.6" refers to the radiated energy field (12.6 × 10¹⁰ J), not kilotons.
- **No seasonal effects**: March/April event frequency matches the annual average.
- **Small downward adjustment** for database reporting lag (late April events may not appear by the May 1 resolution date).

resolution_criteria: This question will resolve as Yes if, after March 10, 2026, and before May 1, 2026, a natural meteor explodes in Earth's atmosphere with a total impact energy of ≥1 kt.
fine_print:
The primary source for this question will be the [NASA Fireball Database](https://cneos.jpl.nasa.gov/fireballs/). If the database is unavailable or incomplete, another source may be used. 

If differences in calculated total impact energy lead to ambiguity, the Fireball Database will take precedence.

If a meteor explodes on Earth's surface, that will qualify as in Earth's atmosphere for purpose of this question.

***
This question's information (resolution criteria, fine print, background info, etc) is synced with an [original identical question](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/42540) which opened on 2026-03-11 17:00:00. This question will resolve based on the resolution criteria and fine print contained above. However, if this question would resolve differently than the original question, then this question will be annulled. Additionally, if the original question's resolution could have been known before this question opened, then this question will be annulled.
